structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: There are three types of financial security that can be required from an offeror, including:

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: offer financial security to protect Canada against loss if an offeror fails to enter into a contract

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: contract financial security to ensure that a contractor fulfills its obligations

structure_blocks.text: payment bond to protect subcontractors and material suppliers

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: Decisions regarding a requirement for financial security for a competitive procurement must be made before you issue the solicitation. Include the appropriate clauses in the solicitation document and resulting contract, and ensure that any mandatory requirements are clearly specified.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: Offerors have the right to choose the form of financial security they will provide. Acceptable forms of financial security include security deposits such as government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit, or surety bonds. Government guaranteed bonds will be valued at their current value.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: Letters of credit received by Canada must state an appropriate expiry date which should extend beyond the anticipated risk period. Therefore, for offer financial security, the expiry date must go beyond the expected contract award date. For contract financial security, the expiry date should go beyond the expected contract completion date. The period before the expiry date should allow you sufficient time to:

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: confirm the offeror/contractor has discharged the obligations for which the letter of credit was provided

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: if the offeror/contractor fails to meet these obligations, to prepare and present the required demand for payment under the letter of credit

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: You must verify that any entity chosen to provide financial security is licensed to do so in the jurisdiction of the contract, as detailed in the Treasury Board Directive on the Management of Procurement .

structure_blocks.heading: Offer financial security
structure_blocks.text: the applicable surety bond. Any deviation must be approved in advance by Legal Services.

structure_blocks.heading: Offer financial security
structure_blocks.text: You must instruct the designated offer receiving unit on how to handle the offer financial securities received.

structure_blocks.heading: Contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: For the successful offeror, you must ensure that offer financial security is not released until the contractor provides the required contract financial security. Decisions on the necessity and the amount of contract financial security should be based on the unique circumstances of the procurement. This should take into account:

structure_blocks.heading: Contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: the type of work and custom of the trade

structure_blocks.heading: Contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: the potential consequences of a contractor’s default

structure_blocks.heading: Contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: the costs versus the risks of providing such security

structure_blocks.heading: Contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: As some businesses may have difficulties obtaining certain forms of security, you should avoid including unreasonable requirements. In some instances, an advance form of security may not be necessary, and instead contract payment holdbacks may be sufficient.

structure_blocks.heading: Contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: Contract financial security must be sought for construction contracts with an estimated value over CAD 500,000.

raw_text:
Financial security

There are three types of financial security that can be required from an offeror, including:

offer financial security to protect Canada against loss if an offeror fails to enter into a contract

contract financial security to ensure that a contractor fulfills its obligations

payment bond to protect subcontractors and material suppliers

Decisions regarding a requirement for financial security for a competitive procurement must be made before you issue the solicitation. Include the appropriate clauses in the solicitation document and resulting contract, and ensure that any mandatory requirements are clearly specified.

Offerors have the right to choose the form of financial security they will provide. Acceptable forms of financial security include security deposits such as government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit, or surety bonds. Government guaranteed bonds will be valued at their current value.

Letters of credit received by Canada must state an appropriate expiry date which should extend beyond the anticipated risk period. Therefore, for offer financial security, the expiry date must go beyond the expected contract award date. For contract financial security, the expiry date should go beyond the expected contract completion date. The period before the expiry date should allow you sufficient time to:

confirm the offeror/contractor has discharged the obligations for which the letter of credit was provided

if the offeror/contractor fails to meet these obligations, to prepare and present the required demand for payment under the letter of credit

You must verify that any entity chosen to provide financial security is licensed to do so in the jurisdiction of the contract, as detailed in the Treasury Board Directive on the Management of Procurement .

See the following sections of the Buyer’s Guide for more information on financial security:

Award: Offer and contract financial security

Manage: Financial security

Offer financial security

When deciding to obtain financial security for an offer, consider:

the potential for prequalifying offerors

the type of work and custom of the trade

the likelihood that offerors will try to withdraw their offers

the consequences if an offeror fails or is unable to enter a contract

The amount of financial security for an offer must be the minimum required to ensure that the offeror enters into contract. Buyers must not require offer financial security for procurements valued at or below CAD 500,000.

To prevent problems in securing any contract financial security, the solicitations must state that failure to provide the contract financial security within the specified timeframe, will result in forfeiture of the offer financial security or a demand for payment under the letter of credit for the offer. The forfeited amount must not exceed the difference between the offer price and the value of the contract with Canada.

The solicitation document must include:

a list of licensed insurance companies capable of providing financial security in the jurisdiction of the contract

the applicable surety bond. Any deviation must be approved in advance by Legal Services.

You must instruct the designated offer receiving unit on how to handle the offer financial securities received.

Contract financial security

For the successful offeror, you must ensure that offer financial security is not released until the contractor provides the required contract financial security. Decisions on the necessity and the amount of contract financial security should be based on the unique circumstances of the procurement. This should take into account:

the type of work and custom of the trade

the potential consequences of a contractor’s default

the costs versus the risks of providing such security

As some businesses may have difficulties obtaining certain forms of security, you should avoid including unreasonable requirements. In some instances, an advance form of security may not be necessary, and instead contract payment holdbacks may be sufficient.

Contract financial security must be sought for construction contracts with an estimated value over CAD 500,000.
